Clauset, Shalizi & Newman 2009 (SIAM Review): MLE + KS power-law fitting methodology (methods anchor)
Forsythe et al. 2011 (British J. Psychology): complexity and fractality jointly predict beauty — indirect support
candidates.computation: Tier A. Canny -> morphological closing -> connected components above a declared area floor -> Clauset MLE power-law fit with KS gate; outputs exponent m, fit quality, hierarchy score = closeness of m to 2 gated on fit, plus scale-cluster count and spacing vs the ~2.7 ratio. Abstains (UNKNOWN) below ~30 elements.
candidates.rationale: Rigorous fitting methodology but theory-grade construct evidence and heuristic element extraction; distinguishes mono-scale minimalism from graded hierarchy, which continuous D cannot.
candidates.collision_with_existing: Clutter proxy = density; fractal D = continuous edge scaling; discrete size distribution of countable elements is new.

candidates.name: water_feature_presence
candidates.construct: Blue-space content: presence/extent of visible water, carrying a preference/restorativeness premium beyond green nature.
literature: White et al. 2010 (J. Environmental Psychology): scenes with water rated higher for preference, affect, perceived restorativeness
Volker & Kistemann 2011 (Int. J. Hygiene & Env. Health): blue-space review — consistent but mostly proxy/self-report-grade associations
Ulrich 1983: water among the 'preferenda' driving rapid positive affect
candidates.computation: Tier A + horizon estimate. Candidates: blue-cyan or specular regions, texturally smooth, wide, below horizon; mandatory reflection test (mirror correlation with the vertically flipped band above the top edge) to reject glass/screens/glossy floors. Tri-state present/absent/ABSTAIN + area fraction.
candidates.rationale: Real literature premium but rare in interiors and false-positive-prone despite the reflection test; abstention path well designed. Low expected yield keeps rank down.
candidates.collision_with_existing: Nothing existing detects water; distinct increment over generic nature content.

candidates.name: facade_pareidolia_salience
candidates.construct: Face-like configural pattern content of walls/facades (pareidolia): obligatory face-detection generalizes to bilaterally symmetric window/door configurations, capturing and holding gaze pre-attentively. Scored as ATTENTION SALIENCE only — the wellbeing extension is Sussman's hypothesis, explicitly not claimed.
literature: Taubert et al. 2017 (Current Biology): pareidolia in monkeys — deep, evolutionarily old mechanism
Liu et al. 2014 (Cortex): illusory faces engage the fusiform face area
Palmer & Clifford 2020 (Psychological Science): pareidolic faces engage genuine social-attention mechanisms (gaze adaptation transfer)
Sussman & Hollander 2015 (Cognitive Architecture): the architectural thesis — largely non-peer-reviewed demonstrations, hence proxy-grade
candidates.computation: Tier A. Max-pooled: (1) Haar frontal-face cascade multi-scale with person regions masked via frozen segmentation (score architecture, not occupants); (2) geometric configuration test on facade planes — co-elevated mirror-symmetric dark-component pairs with a third component below/between inside canonical face proportions. Salience = accepted configurations weighted by angular size and centrality, 0-1. ABSTAIN without a wall/facade plane.
candidates.rationale: Mechanism (pareidolia attention capture) is solidly evidenced, but the architecture-specific literature is largely non-peer-reviewed and the downstream affect link is hypothesis-grade; honest scoping to salience saves it from rejection.
candidates.collision_with_existing: Landmark salience has no notion of configural arrangement; a categorically different (obligatory, gaze-holding) attention channel.

candidates.name: floor_guidance_continuity
candidates.construct: Floor-plane directional cues (material bands, contrast strips, articulated pathways) marking the line of travel — Lynch's continuity in the ground surface, which is where navigating gaze disproportionately rests. Shipped as a hedged proxy: direct experimental evidence is thin.
literature: Lynch 1960: continuity of surface/edge along a path supports imageability
Emo ~2014 (eye-tracking, hedged): wayfinding gaze disproportionately on the floor plane and longest sightlines
Arthur & Passini 1992: circulation should be expressed architecturally incl. floor treatment — design doctrine, explicitly proxy-grade
candidates.computation: Tier A. Isolate floor region; rectify to top-down via VP homography; Hough lines + color-boundary tracing in rectified floor; keep elongated features within ±15 deg of the movement axis; score = (axis-aligned feature length / rectified floor depth) x mean contrast, clipped 0-1, plus 'articulated path present' boolean. ABSTAIN if floor <10% of frame or homography degenerate.
candidates.rationale: Nothing else inspects the floor as an information carrier, but the construct's direct experimental base is admittedly thin (construct + gaze allocation + doctrine) and it rides the VP homography — low rank with the hedge preserved.
candidates.collision_with_existing: Edge clarity and clutter are frame-global; floor-borne directional information is new but weakly evidenced.

candidates.name: patina_wear_index
candidates.construct: Material temporal depth: visible patina/weathering vs pristine newness of natural-material surfaces — a DESCRIPTIVE attribute with deliberate valence abstention (preference literature on aged surfaces is mixed), mirroring the accepted hue-abstention design.
literature: Kellert 2008; Kellert & Calabrese 2015: patina of time as a biophilic attribute — explicitly theory-grade
Nyrud & Bringslimark 2010: wood preference varies with processing/surface character, non-monotone
Broman ~2001 (hedged): appreciation of visible wood features heterogeneous across observers — supports abstention
candidates.computation: Tier A, restricted to regions V12 classifies as natural. Sub-scores: low-frequency chroma variance (weathering gradients), dark low-saturation blob detection (stains), matte-ness (inverse gloss proxy). Combined patina score 0-1 emitted as a descriptive tag with uncertainty flag; the wellbeing layer ABSTAINS on valence.
candidates.rationale: Honest design (explicit valence abstention) but theory-grade construct and purely descriptive output — lowest genuine-new-signal contribution among survivors; kept because abstention-pattern coverage of the Kellert pattern has study value.
candidates.collision_with_existing: No existing attribute captures surface age/wear.

dropped_as_existing: rosenholtz-clutter:edge_density_congestion — duplicates the EXISTING tiled processing-load/clutter proxy in both construct (clutter/processing load as a search-cost correlate) and computation class (multi-scale tiled edge-density statistics); the proposal itself concedes it is the 'weaker but real' member of the family whose value is as a sanity check. Its only novel terms (90th-percentile tile density, Gini concentration) are subsumed by the retained feature_congestion_clutter (V7), which outputs a full per-pixel spatial map, and by subband_entropy_clutter (V6), which the same seat positions as the validated measures. Retaining it would add a third clutter number with no independent construct claim.

V1.reasons: Computability holds: turning-angle/circle-fit decomposition of linked Canny contours is deterministic classical CV and genuinely measures contour shape, which nothing in the existing stack (edge sharpness = definition, fractal D = scaling complexity) carries. Real confounds the implementers must handle but which do not kill it: (a) barrel/lens distortion curves genuinely straight architectural lines — undistort or flag; (b) Canny fragmentation in cluttered photos biases toward short chains, so the minimum-length filter and corner-density denominator need care; (c) the measure conflates architectural contours with object contours (plants, fabric, people) — the person-mask variant honestly drops to AMBER. Evidence base is the real, multi-lab curvature-preference literature; GREEN for the base variant is defensible since it rides no pretrained model. One literature overclaim must be corrected before shipping (see citation concern), but it weakens one anchor, not the construct: Bar & Neta 2006/2007, Dazkir & Read 2012, and the Chuquichambi meta-analysis stand on their own.
V1.citation_concern: Vartanian et al. 2013 (PNAS) is real and did find curvilinear interiors more likely judged beautiful with ACC engagement, but as I recall the paper contour did NOT significantly affect approach-avoidance (enter/exit) decisions, and the predicted amygdala response to rectilinear spaces was not observed. Selling it as 'predict approach decisions' is a misremembered overclaim — strip that clause. Note also Bar & Neta 2007's amygdala finding is for everyday objects, not interiors; fine as mechanism evidence if labeled as such.

V2.reasons: The strongest candidate in the set. Computation is trivially deterministic (windowed FFT, radial average, log-log OLS) and was sandbox-verified on synthetic 1/f^2 noise; the Penacchio & Wilkins 2015 CSF-weighted deviation is an actual published computational model being implemented, not an invented mapping. All five anchors are real and correctly characterized: Field 1987 (JOSA A) 1/f spectra; Fernandez & Wilkins 2008 and Juricevic et al. 2010 (both Perception) linking discomfort to departure from natural spectra with excess mid-SF energy; Graham & Field / Redies et al. on art sharing natural statistics. The declared FOV assumption for cycles/degree is exactly the right honesty move. Residual confound to document, not a kill: the spectrum measures the photograph, not only the room — defocus blur steepens the slope, JPEG compression and downsampling inject/remove high-SF energy, and noise flattens the tail; the implementation should restrict the fitted band away from the Nyquist end and emit an image-quality caveat. GREEN is fair: pure numpy, no pretrained models.

V3.reasons: Construct link is the best-evidenced in environmental psychology (Ulrich 1984 Science and Ulrich et al. 1991 JEP are real and correctly described; Nieuwenhuis et al. 2014 JEP:Applied green-office field experiments and the Bringslimark et al. 2009 review are real, and the heterogeneity is honestly converted into an uncertainty band). Computation is honest about being a proxy: HSV green-band intersected with local-texture floor, white-balance gate, ABSTAIN paths, and the explicit declaration 'plausibly-living green material, not botanically verified.' Known false positives — artificial plants (arguably still partially restorative per the biophilia literature, but unproven), botanical-print wallpaper/fabric (green + textured passes both tests), dark-green upholstery with weave texture — and false negatives (non-green foliage, flowers, autumn tones) are exactly why the claimed AMBER ceiling is correct; do not let anyone promote this to GREEN. Complementarity claim vs C19 is genuine: plan adjacency cannot see the desk-facing-blank-wall inversion.

V4.reasons: The literature is solid (Benedikt 1979 occlusivity, Kaplan & Kaplan 1989, Franz & Wiener 2008, Stamps 2004 meta-analytic heterogeneity, Herzog & Miller 1998 mystery-danger — all real, all correctly hedged) and the intelligibility gate is good design. But the computation as written contains a fabrication by the project's own standard: mystery = occlusivity * log(1+hidden_area) requires flood-filling 'hidden-adjacent walkable area' on a plan grid inferred from ONE photograph. Cells beyond an occluding edge are, by construction, unobserved by the photo-to-plan chain — the inference cannot honestly label them walkable, so hidden_area is either zero or invented. The magnitude term therefore measures the inference engine's willingness to hallucinate rooms, not the building. The occlusivity FRACTION alone survives: classifying isovist boundary segments as occluding vs solid via radial depth-jump discontinuities (with floor visibly continuing past the occluder along nearby rays) is computable from visible evidence at AMBER. Full Benedikt mystery needs a declared floor plan as an extra input.
V4.fixed_computation: Ship two variants. (a) Photo-only, AMBER: raw occlusivity = occluding boundary length / isovist perimeter, where 'occluding' is certified only by visible evidence (radial depth discontinuity with walkable floor visible beyond the occluder on adjacent rays); drop the hidden-area multiplier entirely; keep the C3 intelligibility gate and Stamps uncertainty flag. (b) Declared-input variant: full occlusivity * log(1+hidden_area) computed only when an actual floor plan / occupancy grid is supplied; ABSTAIN otherwise. Never flood-fill cells the single-photo inference has not observed.

V5.reasons: Anchors check out: Ko, Kent, Schiavon, Levitt & Betti 2022 (LEUKOS) view-quality framework with content/access/clarity is real; Markus 1967 (Building Science) on layered-view preference is the standard historical anchor; Ko, Schiavon et al. 2020 (Building and Environment) chamber study did find emotion and working-memory benefits from a window view; Kaplan 2001 (E&B) and EN 17037's layer-based view scoring are correctly cited. The blown-out-aperture case handled as SCORED-window/UNKNOWN-content is the best abstention design in the set — that is the single most common failure of interior photography and it is never guessed. Correct AMBER: rides plane segmentation, homography rectification, and a heuristic window detector. Remaining failure modes to log, not fatal: mirrors, TVs, backlit artwork, and large luminaires can pass the bright-quad-on-wall test (the sky-chroma and depth-discontinuity cross-checks catch some but not all); curtains/blinds and night shots should route to ABSTAIN; a window onto an adjacent lightwell will honestly score low layers, which is arguably correct. Non-duplication claim is accurate — nothing existing scores view content.

V6.reasons: This is the field-standard clutter measure implemented as published, not an invention: Rosenholtz, Li & Nakano 2007 (Journal of Vision) really did validate Subband Entropy against clutter judgments and search performance; Portilla & Simoncelli 2000 and Henderson, Chanceaux & Smith 2009 are real and correctly used; the Reber et al. 2004 fluency bridge is honestly labeled proxy-grade and inherited from machinery already accepted. Steerable pyramid + fixed-bin coefficient entropy with the published luminance-dominant weights is deterministic; GREEN is defensible (no pretrained model). It survives the no-duplication rule only because it is explicitly declared a validation-grade replacement that reports agreement with the legacy tiled proxy — hold it to that: if the two disagree wildly on the corpus, the legacy proxy should be retired, not both shipped as independent signals (double-counting clutter in the hedonics layer would be a silent construct error). Minor confounds shared with all image statistics: resolution, JPEG compression, and depth-of-field blur shift entropy; the corpus-percentile normalization mitigates this only if the corpus is homogeneous in capture quality — state that assumption with the score.

V7.reasons: COMPUTABILITY: the sketch is essentially the actual Rosenholtz Feature Congestion algorithm (Lab covariance ellipsoids for color, band-passed L contrast std, oriented-energy covariance, multiscale Minkowski pooling) — deterministic, well-specified, implementable in numpy/OpenCV without semantics. EVIDENCE: all four anchors are real and correctly characterized: Rosenholtz, Li, Mansfield & Jin 2005 (CHI) is the covariance/ellipsoid clutter paper; Rosenholtz, Li & Nakano 2007 (JoV, 'Measuring visual clutter') did show FC predicting search RT as an effective set-size surrogate; Henderson, Chanceaux & Smith 2009 (JoV) did test clutter measures on real-world scene photographs; Beck, Lohrenz & Trafton 2010 (JEP:Applied) is the local-clutter-around-targets finding that legitimately motivates the map output. This is the best-anchored candidate in the batch. TIER: GREEN is honest — no pretrained models, no depth. COLLISION: the declared 'sharper variant of the ad-hoc tiled clutter proxy' claim is credible because FC is behaviorally validated and locally normalized where the existing proxy is not; recommend retiring or cross-calibrating the old proxy to avoid two clutter numbers disagreeing. RESIDUAL CONFOUNDS to log in metadata, not fatal: sensor noise/high ISO inflates contrast clutter, and exposure/white balance shift the color covariance — mitigate with mild denoising and the stated corpus-percentile normalization. Also verify the exact pooling weights against the 2007 paper's published values (the candidate says 'luminance-dominant'; the published combination is contrast/color-heavy — check before hard-coding).

V8.reasons: EVIDENCE anchors are all real and honestly staged (Oliva & Torralba 2001 IJCV spatial envelope; Torralba & Oliva 2003 Network spectral separability; Kardan et al. 2015 Frontiers showing low-level edge/orientation/color statistics predict perceived naturalness; Berman 2008/Ulrich 1984 correctly demoted to correlational downstream support). But two problems keep this off GREEN. (1) CONSTRUCT TRANSFER: the spectral naturalness axis was derived and validated on whole-scene databases spanning outdoor-natural to urban; using it as a WITHIN-interior gradient is an extrapolation the cited papers do not test. Isotropy is not nature: a cluttered pile of fabric, ornate wallpaper, or wicker furniture is orientation-isotropic; a minimalist plant-free room photographed head-on is maximally cardinal. The measure detects rectilinearity, and only proxies naturalness through it. (2) CAMERA-GEOMETRY CONFOUND: cardinal dominance is measured in image coordinates, so a rectilinear room shot in two-point perspective or with camera roll loses 0/90-degree energy without becoming one iota more natural — the number then measures photographic viewpoint, not the room. TIER: because the fix requires roll/vanishing-point rectification from the existing heuristic geometry stack, and because the interior-transfer of the validation is unproven, the honest ceiling is AMBER, not GREEN.
V8.fixed_computation: Before computing cardinal dominance, estimate camera roll from the vertical vanishing point (already in the stack) and rotate the orientation histogram so 'cardinal' means gravity-aligned, not pixel-aligned; report the isotropy-entropy term (roll-invariant) and cardinal term separately rather than in one fused score; gate per-block scores on a minimum oriented-energy threshold so blank walls do not emit noise-driven entropy; keep the green-hue co-signal capped as stated and validate the fused score against human naturalness ratings on interior photos before promoting past AMBER.

V9.reasons: COMPUTABILITY: trivially yes — a fixed response kernel and a band-coverage fraction over already-computed global and local box-count D, pure numpy, with an honest R^2<0.98 broken-scaling abstention flag. EVIDENCE: Spehar, Clifford, Newell & Taylor 2003 (Computers & Graphics, 'Universal aesthetic of fractals') and Hagerhall, Purcell & Taylor 2004 (JEP, landscape silhouettes, peak ~1.3) are real and correctly cited; Hagerhall et al. 2008 (Perception, EEG alpha response to fractal patterns) is real; Abboushi et al. 2019 (fractal daylight patterns in real interiors) is real and is the key transfer citation; Bies et al. 2016 individual-differences caveat is real and to their credit. TWO CAVEATS that must ride in metadata: (a) the trapezoid falloff constants outside [1.3,1.5] are invented engineering, not literature — fine only because declared; (b) construct stretch: the preference/physiology studies used isolated fractal patterns and silhouettes, whereas box-count D of a whole interior photo's edge map mixes pattern D with scene composition — Abboushi 2019 narrows but does not close this gap. CITATION CONCERN (minor, flag not kill): the 'mid-D reduces physiological stress' claim traces to Taylor-group skin-conductance work that is reported in Taylor 2006 (Leonardo, a perspective piece) rather than a full primary paper — the physiological leg is thinner than the preference leg and the metadata should say 'preference: strong; stress-reduction: preliminary'. TIER: GREEN is fair since it rides existing GREEN image statistics.
V9.citation_concern: Taylor 2006 (Leonardo) is a perspective/review; the skin-conductance stress-reduction result it reports was never published as a full primary study — do not sell the stress leg at the same grade as the Spehar/Hagerhall preference findings.

V10.reasons: COMPUTABILITY: yes — descriptor construction (Lab histogram, LBP, Hu moments, area) and min pairwise chi-square/z-distance over <=6 candidate regions is deterministic classical CV, and the ABSTAIN-below-2-candidates rule plus the repeated-element (colonnade) flag are exactly the right honesty moves. The construct logic is sound: appearance uniqueness among co-visible salient candidates is a defensible within-frame operationalization of Lynch's singularity. EVIDENCE: all three anchors are real and accurately used — Lynch 1960 defines landmark quality as singularity/contrast-with-context; Sorrows & Hirtle 1999 (COSIT) explicitly separate salience from distinctiveness; Raubal & Winter 2002 (GIScience) operationalize landmark suitability as measurable deviation of a candidate's attributes from its neighbors, which is precisely the template followed. The 'within-frame only, not building-wide' scoping is correct and prevents the classic overreach (a frame-unique red door may recur on every floor). TIER: GREEN holds ONLY IF the upstream landmark-salience module is itself classical; if that module rides plane segmentation or any frozen model, this inherits AMBER — state the dependency explicitly in metadata. Minor implementation notes: Hu-moment z-normalization needs a declared reference corpus, and differentiation should be reported alongside candidate count since min-distance over 2 candidates is noisier than over 6.

V11.reasons: EVIDENCE is the strongest part: Conroy Dalton 2003 (Environment and Behavior — the 'follow your nose' VR study showing wayfinders minimize angular deviation) is real; Hillier & Iida 2005 (least-angle segment analysis outpredicting metric/topological distance for movement) is real; Turner 2001 angular segment analysis is the correct formalism. COMPUTABILITY is where the adversarial pressure lands, on two counts. (1) SINGLE-PHOTO PLAN POVERTY: a Tier-B grid inferred from one interior photograph covers roughly one room within the camera frustum; a plan fragment with >=2 genuine degree->=3 decision nodes will be rare, so in practice this abstains on most inputs — acceptable (abstention is honest) but the proposal should say the expected abstention rate is high, and note the cited evidence is building/urban scale while the measurable fragment is room scale. (2) SKELETON FRAGILITY: medial-axis skeletons are notoriously sensitive to boundary noise, and a heuristically inferred occupancy grid has noisy boundaries by construction — spurious spurs manufacture fake degree-3 junctions whose angles are garbage, which would silently corrupt both outputs. This is fixable (see fix), so not a KILL. TIER: AMBER as claimed is exactly right, and the note that it is GREEN-grade on a real supplied plan is correct — recommend also registering it as a declared-input metric for real floor plans, where it will do most of its real work.
V11.fixed_computation: Prune skeleton spurs shorter than k times the local corridor width (distance-transform value at the branch point) before counting decision nodes; require each surviving junction's branches to persist for a minimum metric length before measuring the continuation angle; additionally abstain when the walkable region touches the frustum/occlusion boundary near a junction (the 'junction' may be an artifact of the visibility clip, not the architecture); report node count with the score so downstream consumers can weight by support.

V12.reasons: EVIDENCE: the anchors are real and — unusually for this literature — honestly graded: Tsunetsugu, Miyazaki & Sato 2007 is the genuine 0/45/90% wood-coverage room study (45% most comfortable, non-monotone autonomic response; note it is small-N, ~15 subjects, one culture — the caveat is carried); Nyrud & Bringslimark 2010 is a real review that itself flags the thin evidence base; Fell 2010 is a real UBC dissertation with the skin-conductance office result (dissertation-grade, never fully journal-published — keep that grade visible); Browning et al. 2014 correctly demoted to theory-grade. The dose-response SHAPE claim is legitimately anchored, which is rare. The move of defining the construct as VISUAL wood (so laminate false-positives are bounded proxy error, since the cited responses were visually elicited) is clever and honest. COMPUTABILITY is the weak leg and is why AMBER is the correct ceiling on two independent grounds: it rides plane segmentation (automatic AMBER per house rules), and the wood/stone classifier is genuinely fragile — brown painted walls, brick, cork, leather, and striped wallpaper share the wood hue band; striped or grained non-wood textiles pass the single-dominant-orientation grain test; stone-vs-concrete-vs-plaster are near-indistinguishable to saturation+granularity features. Without a precision check this becomes a fabrication vector for exactly the sin the panel exists to catch.
V12.fixed_computation: Before shipping, run the classifier on a small labeled interior set (e.g., 100 hand-labeled planes) and publish per-class precision/recall in the metric metadata; emit a per-plane confidence and ABSTAIN on planes below threshold rather than force-labeling; require agreement between the hue/texture classifier and the existing acoustic material-table label where both exist, and flag disagreements instead of averaging; apply the 0.45-centered inverted-U only to wood_fraction (as cited), not to the combined natural_material_fraction, since the dose-response evidence is wood-specific.

V13.reasons: Computable exactly as written: Sobel orientation histogram entropy and the Redies-lab pairwise (second-order) orientation entropy are pure deterministic pixel arithmetic, no segmentation, no pretrained models — GREEN is honest. The anchors are real and are the right ones: Grebenkina et al. 2018 (Frontiers) genuinely reports first/second-order edge-orientation entropy predicting liking of man-made images, and the Redies/Brachmann line on near-maximal edge-orientation entropy in artworks is real. It measures a genuinely unmeasured axis (orientation distributional structure vs gradient strength). One defensible caveat to record, not a kill: orientation entropy of an interior photo is sensitive to camera viewpoint (one-point vs two-point perspective concentrates orientations differently) and lens distortion, so it is a photo statistic that only proxies the room; the validating literature was itself photo-based, so this is a shared limitation, but the writeup should state the viewpoint sensitivity explicitly. Feeding the raw statistic to the hedonics layer rather than baking in a response shape is the correct honest design.

V14.reasons: The anchors are real: Van Den Wymelenberg & Inanici 2014 and 2016 (LEUKOS) did find luminance percentile/ratio metrics outperforming illuminance metrics for comfort/preference, and the proposal correctly hedges the 2016 paper. The dominant failure mode is honestly confronted: an auto-exposed, tone-mapped 8-bit JPEG partially manufactures the very skew/kurtosis/tail structure being measured — camera processing, not the room, can set the histogram shape. The clipping flags and >10% ABSTAIN handle the censoring case but NOT the exposure-normalization case (a well-exposed photo of a gloomy room and of a comfortable room can have similar log-Y shapes); the AMBER ceiling and 'ordinal proxy' framing are exactly right and must not drift upward. The comfort-band scoring direction (bounded non-uniformity preferred, gloom and heavy bright tail penalized) is consistent with the cited findings. Minor overclaim: the Amundadottir et al. 2017 paper is about non-visual health potential, visual interest and gaze — it supports 'distribution structure matters' only loosely, and is being stretched slightly as the third leg.
V14.citation_concern: Amundadottir, Rockcastle, Sarey Khanie & Andersen 2017 is real but its finding is about non-visual health potential / visual interest / gaze, not a direct 'distribution structure beats mean/variance for comfort' result — mildly overclaimed as support here.

V15.reasons: Computation is genuinely deterministic (64x64 neighbor differences, dark-mask connected components) and fixes a named, real blindness: brightness variance is shuffle-invariant, and nothing in the stack measures shadow structure. Rockcastle & Andersen 2014 (Building and Environment) really did construct spatial-contrast metrics to separate daylight compositions with identical global statistics, and the later Rockcastle/Amundadottir/Andersen work (e.g., the 2017 Lighting Research & Technology contrast-measures paper) really correlated such measures with rated interest/excitement — the '~2015-2017, years hedged' honesty is appropriate. AMBER is the correct ceiling and must stay: validation used HDR renderings under controlled adaptation; on an LDR photo both the neighbor-difference statistic and the dark mask (Y < 0.2 median) are exposure- and tone-curve-dependent, so shadow_coherence partly measures the camera. The single-frame adaptation of a metric originally designed for temporal/spatial daylight dynamics should be stated as an adaptation, not the validated instrument itself.

V16.reasons: The evidence base is the strongest in the batch and correctly cited: Chamilothori, Chinazzo, Rodrigues, Dan-Glauser, Wienold & Andersen 2019 (Building and Environment 150) is a real study with subjective AND physiological responses to sunlight-pattern geometry in validated VR; the LEUKOS VR-adequacy validation (Chamilothori, Wienold & Andersen 2019) and the later cross-regional replication are real, and the hedging on the latter's year is honest. Computation is mostly sound and the abstention design (no hard-edged patch -> geometry sub-scores abstain; clipping flag) is careful. Note in its favor: LDR clipping is less fatal here than for V14 because patch BOUNDARY geometry (P^2/4πA, fragmentation) survives clipping. Residual failure modes to log: specular reflections, white objects on dark grounds, and hard-edged electric-light pools can pass the threshold+hard-edge test and masquerade as sun patches — a false-positive rate the composite should carry as an uncertainty flag; and the floor/wall restriction rides plane segmentation, which alone forces AMBER. Claimed ceiling matches reality.

V17.reasons: The computation is fully deterministic classical CV (Lab + DoG orientation energies, Gaussian-windowed local mean/covariance, Tikhonov-regularized Mahalanobis) with no pretrained models, so GREEN is defensible, and reporting at two window sizes honestly handles the unknown-FOV problem (the '1.5-deg window' constant is otherwise unknowable from a bare photo — keep the dual-scale reporting mandatory, not optional). Anchors are real: Duncan & Humphreys 1989 is exactly the covariance-normalized-similarity account, and Rosenholtz, Li & Nakano 2007 Feature Congestion is literally this denominator. One construct-level overclaim to trim: the statistic measures whether SOMETHING pops out, not whether a wayfinding-relevant thing (exit, sign, hazard) does — the max-D element may be a plant or a poster. Sold as 'signal-to-clutter margin of the most conspicuous element,' object-free, it is honest and genuinely complementary to landmark salience; sold as 'predicts finding of exits,' it fabricates task relevance the pixels cannot certify.
V17.citation_concern: Rosenholtz 1999 (Vision Research) is primarily the MOTION-popout statistical saliency paper; the extension of the SD-from-local-distribution model to static color/orientation search is Rosenholtz 2001 (Perception & Psychophysics). Minor slippage — the model is hers, but the year/venue attached to the static claim is off by one paper.

V18.reasons: The geometry is clean and the anchors are real (Appleton 1975; Dawes & Ostwald's isovist-based prospect-refuge analyses of Wright interiors; Stamps 2005 on enclosure and perceived safety), but the computation as sketched fails the fabrication test at Tier B: the REAR hemifield is the one region a single forward-facing photograph cannot observe. The inferred occupancy grid is built from planes/depth visible in the ~60-90 degree forward FOV; every cell behind the camera is unobserved, so 'median rear radial' — the entire refuge term, the half of the construct that is new — would be computed over hallucinated space. This is the same class of error as the glass-house inversion: the metric's defining input is structurally outside the sensor. The proposed ABSTAIN condition (VP recovery fails) guards the wrong failure. Front-hemifield prospect alone survives, but that duplicates the existing prospect/view-depth attribute, which the proposal's own collision note concedes.
V18.fixed_computation: Recompute only when camera position + view axis can be located within a DECLARED plan (real floor plan or seat/vantage map — an already-recognized extra-input class), where rear radials are grounded in observed geometry; then the front/rear balance formula is fine as written. At pure Tier B, the honest behavior is: rear/refuge and balance ABSTAIN whenever any rear-hemifield radial crosses unobserved grid cells (which from one photo is always), reporting front prospect only — i.e., no new attribute.

V19.reasons: The evidence base is exactly right: Stamps (2005, Environment and Behavior, 'Visual permeability, locomotive permeability, safety, and enclosure') really did dissociate the two channels experimentally — spaciousness tracked visual permeability, judged safety tracked locomotive permeability — and the E&PB 2005 isovist/enclosure formalization is real. The collision claim is also honest: a single solid-angle enclosure index provably cannot distinguish a glass box (visually permeable, locomotively sealed) from a doorway-riddled masonry room. Tier B locomotive permeability is cleanly computable on the plan grid (occluding-edge classification against walkable cells is standard isovist bookkeeping). The weak link is Tier A glazing detection: glass is the canonical classical-CV failure surface — monocular depth nets are unstable on glass/mirrors, night windows read as dark solids, curtains and mirrors false-negative/false-positive, and 'bright wall region' is confounded by wall-washed painted walls. The two channels abstaining independently is the right honest design, but the Tier A channel will have a high error rate on real photos and should abstain aggressively (e.g., when the glazing evidence is luminance-only with no depth-discontinuity corroboration). AMBER is correct since both channels ride plane segmentation/depth.
V19.fixed_computation: Tier A visual_permeability should require two independent cues per candidate region (luminance anomaly AND depth-beyond-plane discontinuity, or sky/vegetation content) before counting area as glazed; luminance-only candidates go to UNKNOWN rather than into the ratio.

V20.reasons: All four anchors are real and correctly characterized: Weisman 1981 (E&B, architectural legibility — visual access as a predictor of disorientation), Wiener/Hölscher/Büchner/Konieczny 2012 (Psychological Research — gaze and route choice biased toward the option with the longest line of sight at decision points), Braaksma & Cook 1980 (visibility index in transportation terminals — a genuine deterministic-visibility precedent), Peponis/Zimring/Choi 1990 (E&B, 'Finding the building in wayfinding'). The per-node MINIMUM-branch preview is genuinely not in C1 (global mean integration) or camera prospect, and the computation (skeleton graph, degree>=3 nodes, ray bundles per branch) is straightforward deterministic geometry on a grid. The one honest caveat the proposal underplays: a plan grid inferred from ONE interior photograph almost never contains degree>=3 junctions — the inferred grid is basically one room plus fragments — so in pure Tier-B-from-photo mode this metric will ABSTAIN nearly always and only earns its keep when a declared floor plan is supplied. That is acceptable under the abstention discipline (same situation as several C-metrics) but should be stated. AMBER for the inferred grid is correct; on a declared measured plan the geometry itself would be GREEN-grade, so the ceiling should be documented as input-dependent.
V20.fixed_computation: Document expected abstention behavior: on single-photo inferred grids, emit ABSTAINED (no decision nodes) rather than a degenerate score from spurious skeleton branches; add a minimum-branch-length filter so skeletonization artifacts on noisy inferred grids don't manufacture fake junctions.

V21.reasons: The construct is sound and genuinely missing (enclosure is lateral, prospect is depth; neither isolates verticality), and the computation is feasible: with three orthogonal Manhattan VPs you can recover focal length and pitch from the image itself, and a height-to-width RATIO is dimensionless, so the notorious monocular metric-scale ambiguity does not apply. Real failure modes remain — wide-angle real-estate lenses and shots where full floor-to-ceiling extent or opposing walls are not visible — but the declared ABSTAIN covers them, and AMBER for VP/Manhattan reliance is correct. The problem is the evidence column. First anchor is misattributed: Vartanian et al. 2013 (PNAS) is the CONTOUR study (curvilinear vs rectilinear); the ceiling-height x enclosure factorial is Vartanian et al. 2015, Journal of Environmental Psychology ('Architectural design and the brain: effects of ceiling height and perceived enclosure...'). The proposal effectively cites the same 2015 paper twice (once mislabeled as 2013 PNAS, once hedged as '~2015'). Second, Meyers-Levy & Zhu 2007 is real (JCR) but it is a consumer-behavior priming study from a literature with poor replication credibility; it should be carried as a suggestive secondary anchor with an explicit uncertainty flag, not as load-bearing construct validity. With the citation fixed, the Vartanian 2015 factorial plus the openness/approach findings are sufficient.
V21.citation_concern: 'Vartanian et al. 2013 (PNAS)' as a ceiling-height factorial is wrong — 2013 PNAS is contour, not ceiling height; the correct anchor is Vartanian et al. 2015, J. Environmental Psychology. Meyers-Levy & Zhu 2007 is real but is priming-literature evidence and should be flagged as low-replication-confidence, not sold as established.

V22.reasons: Anchors check out: Cuttle 1971 ('Lighting patterns and the flow of light', Lighting Research & Technology) introduced the vector/scalar illuminance ratio as the correlate of perceived flow of light; the ~1.2-1.8 pleasant-modelling range matches Cuttle's Lighting by Design guidance and is properly hedged; Flynn et al. 1973 (J.IES) is the classic diffuse-vs-directional impressions study. The proposal is also honest that true vector illuminance is unmeasurable from a photo and that this is a gradient-based proxy — that candor plus the inverted-U mapping consistent with the existing fluency machinery is the right shape. Computability concerns are real but survivable: (a) lens vignetting produces radial luminance gradients that will masquerade as directional light on large planes — this is a photography confound, not a room property; (b) HDR/tone-mapped real-estate photography flattens gradients, so heavily processed photos will systematically read 'diffuse'; (c) auto white balance and albedo texture add noise, though normalizing the gradient by mean Y and requiring high R^2 coherence mitigates. Because the confounds are exposure-pipeline artifacts rather than semantics gaps, they can be partially controlled. AMBER (rides plane segmentation + VP homography, plus photography confounds) is the correct ceiling.
V22.fixed_computation: Add a vignetting guard: fit and reject a purely radial (image-centered) gradient component before attributing directionality to the scene; and abstain or flag UNCERTAIN when multiple planes disagree on inferred light direction (incoherent fits = processed photo, not diffuse light).

V23.reasons: Strongest of the six on the computability axis. Flynn et al. 1973 genuinely showed that luminance PLACEMENT (peripheral/wall emphasis vs overhead) shifts spaciousness and pleasantness impressions of the same room; Flynn 1977 (LD+A) is real and appropriately hedged; Loe, Mansfield & Rowlands 1994 (LR&T, 'Appearance of lit environment...') really did find that luminance in the horizontal 40-degree band drives lightness/interest judgments. The computation is a trivial deterministic ratio over already-existing plane labels; exposure cancels in the ratio; and the usual albedo objection does not bite here because Flynn's construct is about perceived luminance distribution regardless of whether it comes from lamps or reflectance — a dark-painted wall genuinely IS low wall luminance for the percept. Orthogonality to the vertical-illuminance magnitude proxy is correctly argued. Residual risks: misclassified planes (tall furniture counted as wall) and windows on walls inflating wall luminance with source rather than surface light — the latter is worth masking. AMBER solely because it rides plane segmentation; the arithmetic itself is GREEN-grade.
V23.fixed_computation: Exclude detected window/glare regions (from the existing glare mask, or V24 if adopted) from wall-plane pixels before computing wall dominance, so a bright window doesn't read as a wall-washed wall.

V24.reasons: Evidence is real and correctly pitched: Keep, James & Inman 1980 (Anaesthesia) did report roughly doubled delirium/hallucination incidence in a windowless vs windowed ICU (correlational unit comparison, properly not sold as an RCT); Verderber 1986 (E&B, person-window transactions in hospital environments) is real; Ulrich et al. 2008 (HERD evidence-based design review) is real and 'repeatedly associated' is honest wording. The gap claim is correct — windows currently exist in the stack only as light sources or plan distances, never as presence/size in the visual field. The danger is the detection heuristic, and it matters more here than elsewhere because the WB payload rides on a BINARY any_window flag: 'bright quadrilateral on a wall' matches TVs, backlit art, whiteboards, doorways into bright rooms; and a windowed room photographed at night reads windowless. A false any_window=true on a whiteboard is exactly the fabrication class the panel exists to kill. The proposal already gestures at the fix (sky/vegetation content from V5/V3) — it must be made mandatory, not optional. With that gating and abstention on suspected night/artificial-only scenes, this is a defensible, outcome-anchored WB attribute. AMBER (plane segmentation + content detectors) is correct.
V24.fixed_computation: Make the binary any_window flag require corroboration: luminance-anomaly candidates count only if they also show sky/vegetation content OR a depth-beyond-wall-plane discontinuity; luminance-only candidates contribute to a hedged WWR_candidate with an uncertainty flag, and the whole predicate ABSTAINS when scene cues indicate night/artificial-only lighting (otherwise windowed-at-night rooms are scored falsely windowless).

V25.reasons: Survives, with caveats. The two literatures are real and honestly kept apart (vitality vs. event-boundary memory purge), and thresholds genuinely are invisible to C4. Computability concerns that must stay on the label: (a) a single-photo inferred plan grid covers one visible room, so 'thresholds per 10 m of spine' is a density estimate from a severely truncated spine — high-variance, small-sample; (b) the Tier-A corroborator (floor-connected rectangles, aspect ~1:2.2) will fire on tall windows, wardrobes, and framed panels, so it must stay a downweighting corroborator, never a scorer; (c) Gehl's doors-per-100m criterion is an EXTERIOR street-frontage standard for retail units — porting it to interior circulation is an analogical proxy and should be flagged as such in the output, not presented as a validated interior finding. AMBER is correct (plan inference + heuristic door detection).
V25.citation_concern: Gehl 2010 and Radvansky 2006/2011 and Zacks & Swallow 2007 are all real. Mild overclaim on Ellard 2015: the lively NYC condition (BMW Guggenheim Lab blank-facade study) confounded permeability with visual complexity, activity, and food smells — 'specifically permeable' is stronger than the study supports.
V25.fixed_computation: Emit a field-of-view coverage fraction alongside the density and widen the reported CI when the visible spine is <~8-10 m; demote Tier-A detection to corroborator-only (already proposed) and require floor-plane contact plus traversable gap in the grid before counting.

V26.reasons: Construct is real and genuinely orthogonal to C4 (discriminability of options vs. count of options), and the junction-only abstention is exactly the right discipline. Two failure modes need explicit handling before I'd defend it: (a) pixel starvation — the 'visible interior' through a distant opening is often <100x50 px, where LBP and chi-square histograms are noise-dominated; (b) illumination confound — one backlit corridor vs. one front-lit identical corridor scores as 'differentiated' when the architecture is identical; exposure gradients across a wide-angle frame will systematically inflate dissimilarity. Note this metric therefore measures MOMENTARY visual discriminability, not persistent architectural differentiation — that is still encoding-relevant (Weisman's construct is perceptual), but the docstring must say it. Expect the predicate to fire on a small minority of photos, which is honest but means low coverage.
V26.citation_concern: Weisman 1981 (E&B legibility factors) and Lynch 1960 are solid. O'Neill 1991 is slightly mis-sold: his E&B work manipulated floor-plan complexity (ICD) and signage, not perceptual differentiation of route options per se — the differentiation-performance link belongs to Weisman (and Evans' legibility reviews), not O'Neill. Fix the attribution rather than the design.
V26.fixed_computation: Gate each opening on minimum aperture area (e.g. >=2000 px and >=40 px width) else drop it from the pairwise set; compute dissimilarity on exposure-normalized Lab (subtract per-opening luminance mean before histogramming, keep a separate raw-luminance term with a lighting-confound flag); ABSTAIN if fewer than 2 openings survive the gate.

V27.reasons: The strongest evidence base in the batch — signage is the one experimentally validated wayfinding intervention, and pairing it with C4 (high decision load + no visible signage) is the actionable output. MSER+SWT is genuine classical CV (Epshtein et al. 2010) and computable as sketched. Two problems, one of which is a design flaw as written: (a) FALSE POSITIVES — in interiors, SWT fires on posters, whiteboards, monitors, book spines, and wall art; the proposal admits it cannot distinguish these, so 'summed conspicuity' will be inflated in text-rich offices and the metric partly measures text clutter, not wayfinding support; (b) the zero-candidate case scoring 0 is wrong — a photo not aimed at a decision point punishes the building for camera direction. A single frame samples the signage SYSTEM so poorly that 0-with-caveat still reads as a finding. AMBER is correct.
V27.citation_concern: O'Neill 1991, Weisman 1981, Arthur & Passini 1992 are all real and correctly characterized; the 'largest gains in least-legible plans' interaction is a fair reading of O'Neill.
V27.fixed_computation: Change the zero-candidate branch to ABSTAIN (UNKNOWN) unless the frame is classified as a junction/decision view (reuse V26's junction gate or C4's plan-side decision points); report max conspicuity rather than summed as the headline (sum rewards text clutter); mask candidates on screen/monitor planes and require wall/ceiling plane attachment plus eye-height-band placement before a candidate counts toward the score.

V28.reasons: Computable exactly as sketched (band extraction from VP horizon, tiled edge density / orientation entropy / RMS contrast — all deterministic), and the low-end valence genuinely complements the global clutter proxy. But two honesty repairs are required: (a) the ENTIRE behavioral evidence base is exterior streetscape/facade work (Ellard's blank-facade field study, Gehl's ground-floor articulation, Cavalcante's streetscape statistics) — porting it to interior corridor walls is a proxy transfer the proposal does not flag; interiors lack the pedestrian-transit framing that drives those effects; (b) the wellbeing chain is two-hop: blank band -> boredom (Ellard, small-N, self-report + EDA) and separately boredom -> cortisol (Merrifield & Danckert, lab induction with films, nothing architectural) — chaining them into 'autonomic under-stimulation cost' of a wall is overclaimed and should be reported as a speculative link, not an anchored one. Also note edge density and RMS contrast are confounded by focus, exposure, and shooting distance — corpus normalization helps but a defocused background wall will read as 'dead'. AMBER correct.
V28.citation_concern: All four citations are real (Ellard 2015; Gehl, Kaefer & Reigstad 2006 'Close Encounters with Buildings'; Cavalcante et al. 2014 PLoS ONE; Merrifield & Danckert 2014). The problem is not invention but transfer: exterior-facade findings sold for interior walls, and a lab boredom-cortisol result chained onto it without saying the chain is inferential.
V28.fixed_computation: Label the construct 'streetscape-derived proxy, interior generalization untested' in the metric metadata; report the boredom-cortisol link as SPECULATIVE tier regardless of overall AMBER; add a depth/defocus sanity gate (skip tiles whose local sharpness is below a frame-relative floor) so out-of-focus walls abstain rather than score dead.

V29.reasons: Edge coverage and edge continuity are cleanly computable from the plan grid and measure something C11/C16 cannot (locomotion-time protection vs. stationary refuge). But as written the third component breaks: the center-forcing index requires sampled entry-entry / entry-seat shortest paths, and a single-photo inferred plan grid mostly CANNOT see the entries (they are behind the camera or outside the frustum) and has no seat map unless one is declared. Implemented literally, the entry set would be guessed, making that component fabrication-adjacent — the cardinal sin. Also flag the construct transfer honestly: Kallai's thigmotaxis findings come from Morris-type arena navigation in novel/anxiogenic virtual environments; extrapolating to everyday rooms is a plausibility argument reinforced by Gehl's observational edge effect, not a demonstrated interior mechanism. AMBER correct (fully downstream of plan inference).
V29.citation_concern: Kallai et al. 2005/2007, Gehl 2011, Appleton 1975 are all real and correctly characterized (the anxiety link is correlational, which the proposal states). No invented anchors; the arena-to-room generalization should be flagged as a proxy.
V29.fixed_computation: Split the composite: emit coverage and continuity from the grid alone (SCORED); compute center-forcing ONLY when entries/seats are available — from declared seat/entry maps or a grid whose doorway detections are high-confidence — otherwise that component ABSTAINS and the composite is the mean of available components with an explicit components-used field. Never synthesize entry points.

V30.reasons: The best proposal in the batch and the easiest to defend: zero new image processing, deterministic arithmetic on the existing box-count table, sandbox-verified with a correct positive control (Sierpinski -> 1.0) and a correct negative control (single-scale grid gets a fittable but incoherent 'D'). Its primary value is as a VALIDITY GUARD on the existing fractal D — Taylor, Micolich & Jonas's methodological point (a slope is only a fractal dimension if it holds across the scaling range) is exactly right and the current metric silently violates it. One caveat to encode: photographs give a short scaling range (~1.5 decades) and octave-slope variance is nonzero even for genuine fractal scenes due to finite-size and binarization-threshold effects, so calibrate the coherence floor on the corpus rather than treating 1.0 as attainable; the coherence number itself partly reflects resolution and thresholding, not only architecture. The proposal's self-imposed AMBER (construct link to Salingaros/Alexander is theory-grade, not empirical) is the honest call and should be kept — as a guard it is GREEN-grade, as a coherence construct it is AMBER.
V30.citation_concern: None. Salingaros & West 1999 (Environment and Planning B), Taylor, Micolich & Jonas 1999 (Nature, Pollock drip-painting fractal analysis), and Alexander 2001 are all real and correctly labeled theory-grade where they are theory-grade.

V31.reasons: Computable as sketched and honestly AMBER (rides depth + plane segmentation). Two real weaknesses must be declared. (a) Construct validity: every anchor is a LANDSCAPE/forest study; no interior mystery study is cited because the interior literature is thin, and Herzog's own later work (Herzog & Smith 1988; Herzog & Flynn-Smith 2001) shows mystery and DANGER load together in enclosed/urban settings — a dim bending corridor can be a threat cue, not a preference cue. The output should be reported as a mystery-CUE score with valence explicitly unresolved for interiors, not as a preference predictor. (b) Computability confound: wide-angle/phone lenses barrel-distort straight floor-wall boundaries into curves, so cue (1) can score the LENS rather than the corridor; the occlusion and lit-aperture cues are sound in principle but mirrors, TVs, and bright framed art will fire the aperture detector unless the depth-discontinuity threshold is strict. Abstention rules are appropriately conservative. Defensible to Kirsh only with the valence hedge stated in the construct text.
V31.fixed_computation: Before measuring boundary curvature, undistort using EXIF lens metadata or run a straight-line consistency check on known-straight structures (door frames, ceiling grid); if neither is available, drop cue (1) and score from cues (2)+(3) only, flagging the reduction. Require the lit-aperture cue to show BOTH a depth discontinuity beyond the wall plane and luminance consistent with an illuminated volume (not a specular/emissive flat patch) to exclude mirrors and screens.
V31.citation_concern: Anchors are real (Kaplan & Kaplan 1989; Herzog & Bryce 2007 is a within-forest-settings study; Gimblett, Itami & Fitzgibbon 1985, Landscape Journal) but all are landscape studies — selling them as validation for interior corridors overreaches; and the cited corpus omits Herzog's mystery-danger findings, which cut against a positive-valence reading indoors.

V32.reasons: Strongest of the six. All four anchors are real and on-construct: Kaplan & Kaplan 1989 coherence; Kotabe, Kardan & Berman 2016 (JEP: General) genuinely shows low-level spatial features drive perceived disorder independent of semantics, which is precisely the license needed for a semantics-free operationalization; Stamps 2004 is the meta-analysis separating coherence from complexity; Coburn et al. 2020 (Cortex) does recover coherence as a principal dimension of architectural experience. Computation is classical CV (SLIC + fixed-threshold agglomerative clustering), deterministic with fixed seed, and the three components (few clusters, contiguity, repetition) map onto 'hangs together / organization / redundancy' rather than mere quantity, so the collision defense against the clutter proxy and palette entropy is genuine. Honest self-cap at AMBER for proxy status is correct even though no frozen model is involved — the construct is rating-defined and the pipeline is untested against ratings. Residual confounds worth logging, not killing: shallow depth-of-field and exposure choices homogenize texture (photography style leaks into the score), and the fixed clustering threshold will need corpus calibration; the rectilinear sub-score should abstain rather than score near-zero for legitimately non-Manhattan interiors.

V33.reasons: The construct-to-measure link is the most direct in the batch: Franz & Wiener 2008 and Wiener & Franz 2005 really did correlate isovist jaggedness with rated spatial complexity in controlled (VR) interiors, and P²/4πA is exactly their scalar. The danger is entirely in the substrate: on a photo-INFERRED rasterized plan grid, perimeter is inflated by segmentation noise and rasterization stair-stepping, so raw J measures pipeline noise before it measures room shape — P²/A is quadratically sensitive to boundary jitter. The sketch anticipates this (Douglas-Peucker before the reflex-vertex count) but computes J itself on what is implied to be the raw polygon. Second honesty point: an isovist recovered from one photograph covers only the visible cone; occluded pockets — the very alcoves that make real isovists jagged — are unobservable, so Tier-B-from-photo J is a lower bound and must be labeled as such (this is fine when the plan grid comes from a real declared floor plan). AMBER cap is correct; dependency on V4's isovist is declared.
V33.fixed_computation: Compute J on the Douglas-Peucker-simplified polygon (same epsilon = 1 grid cell), not the raw rasterized boundary, and report sensitivity across epsilon in {0.5, 1, 2} cells; if log(J) is not stable across that range, ABSTAIN. Label photo-inferred results 'visible-cone lower bound' distinct from declared-plan results.

V34.reasons: The literature is real and the response shape is right, but the computation as sketched does not measure dilapidation — it measures 'things on walls.' The wall-plane module (illumination-model residual blobs = stains; high-frequency edge clusters on 'nominally smooth' planes = cracks) will fire on posters, whiteboards, art, signage, outlets, switches, vents, wall-washing light gradients, and hard shadows, all of which are low-frequency luminance deviations or edge clusters on wall planes. Distinguishing a water stain from a shadow or a framed print requires exactly the semantic judgment classical CV cannot deliver, and 'nominally smooth' presupposes material knowledge the pipeline does not have. In an ordinary occupied office photo, false positives would dominate the anomaly-area fraction, so the output would be a confident-looking dilapidation number that is fabricated with respect to its construct — the cardinal sin, and acknowledging the confound in the rationale does not remove it from the number. The one genuinely computable fragment is the suspended-ceiling module, where the regular grid supplies a strong structural prior and per-tile deviation (discolored/missing tiles) is a real, checkable signal.
V34.fixed_computation: Salvage as a narrow 'ceiling-condition index': detect suspended-ceiling grids via regular Hough spacing; score per-tile luminance/chroma outliers against the tile population within the same plane (sagging/missing/stained tiles), masking luminaires and diffusers by their regular positions and specular/emissive signature; ABSTAIN on all other surfaces and whenever no tile grid is confidently detected. Wall stain/crack scoring should be dropped entirely, or gated behind instance-segmentation object masks AND a very high anomaly threshold with the score renamed 'unmasked wall anomaly fraction (uninterpreted)'.
V34.citation_concern: Anchors are real (Kim & de Dear 2013 B&E nonlinear dissatisfier finding; Leaman & Bordass 1999 forgiveness; Vilnai-Yavetz, Rafaeli & Schneider Yaacov 2005; Wilson & Kelling 1982 correctly quarantined as mechanism-only). The problem is not the citations — it is that none of them validate an image-residual operationalization; they are all survey/POE, so even the salvaged version is proxy-level.

V35.reasons: Evidence base is the best-established environmental-psych literature in the batch — Oldham & Rotchford 1983, Oldham & Fried 1987, Brennan, Chugh & Kline 2002, Kim & de Dear 2013 are all real, on-construct, and correctly characterized as satisfaction/withdrawal findings rather than oversold as stress causation. The design's second use (independent photographic cross-check on plan-derived C12, with disagreement itself surfaced as a Tier-B inference-quality flag) is genuinely good epistemic engineering, not duplication. Three computability caveats keep it AMBER and need to be explicit in the payload: (a) monocular metric scale — 'per 100 m²' is proxy-scaled and should be reported ordinally or with a declared scale anchor (standard desk height 720-750mm is a defensible anchor, but say which); (b) the visible cone is a biased sample of the floor — one photographed corner does not estimate workplace density, only local visible density; (c) chairs alone conflate meeting rooms with workstation zones — a room of 12 chairs around one table is not 12 workstations. The classical edge-autocorrelation fallback is nearly worthless and is honestly flagged as such; fine as long as its confidence tier is machine-readable, not just prose.
V35.fixed_computation: Define a workstation as a desk instance with a co-located chair OR monitor (spatial association on the back-projected floor plane), not raw chair count; report nearest-neighbor spacing in desk-height-normalized units unless a metric anchor is declared; label the output 'visible-cone local density' to block silent generalization to the whole floor.

V36.reasons: The vision-science anchors are all real and correctly deployed: Bouma 1970 (critical spacing ≈ 0.5× eccentricity), Balas/Nakano/Rosenholtz 2009 and Rosenholtz et al. 2012 (summary-statistic account of crowding and search), Freeman & Simoncelli 2011 (eccentricity-scaled pooling metamers), Whitney & Levi 2011. The computation (annular pooling regions, orientation circular variance + Lab covariance volume, area-weighted sum beyond ~2 deg) is deterministic and implementable. Two honesty conditions: (a) the eccentricity geometry is only meaningful given the photo's angular subtense — a constant-FOV assumption makes the score a function of the photographer's lens and crop, not the room; a phone ultrawide vs a 50mm shot of the same vantage would score differently. EXIF focal length exists for most photos and should gate the computation. (b) The construct claim ('predicts noticing signage/landmarks you are not fixating') is a mechanistic extrapolation from psychophysics with letter/synthetic stimuli to real interiors — no cited study validates it in architectural scenes, so the payload should call it a mechanism-grounded load index, not a validated wayfinding predictor. With those stated, it is defensible as the stack's only eccentricity-aware attribute, and the fixation default (VP) is a reasonable, declared convention for corridor/wayfinding vantages.
V36.fixed_computation: Use EXIF focal length + sensor size to compute degrees-per-pixel when available; when EXIF is absent, either ABSTAIN or emit the score with an explicit 'FOV-assumed' uncertainty flag at reduced confidence rather than silently applying the constant.

V37.reasons: Skewness and PCA-eccentricity of radials are deterministic, cheap, and genuinely distinct from C24 (magnitude) and prospect (axial). The one real computability hole: a plan grid inferred from ONE photograph only covers the camera frustum, so the 'isovist' is a partial sector, not Benedikt's 360-degree polygon. Elongation computed on a frustum-clipped polygon is dominated by the lens FOV and camera heading, not the room — a square room shot with a wide lens down its diagonal will score elongated. Survives only if the metric is explicitly declared a VISIBLE-SECTOR statistic (normalized within the reconstructed sector) or is computed on a supplied full plan; with that relabeling the anchors carry it. Benedikt 1979 does define radial variance/skewness as core descriptors; Franz & Wiener 2008 (Env & Planning B) did relate isovist statistics to experiential ratings in VR; Stamps's spaciousness/elongation work exists and is properly hedged. AMBER is the correct ceiling (rides plane-seg/depth plan inference).
V37.fixed_computation: Compute skewness/elongation only over the reconstructed visible sector, normalize elongation by sector aspect (or report per-degree radial profile), and emit SECTOR-ONLY flag; full-polygon variant requires a declared floor plan. Never report as a 360-degree isovist property from Tier B.
V37.citation_concern: Minor: the VR spaciousness-rating result is closer to Franz, von der Heyde & Bulthoff 2005 than Franz & Wiener 2008 (which is about behavioral relevance of isovist measures); same lab, right construct, slightly misremembered paper.

V38.reasons: The construct is real and the anchors check out (drift is Conroy Dalton's measure; the OmniVista symposium paper and the 2001 UCL thesis exist; Wiener/Franz et al. 2007 in Perception is real). But the headline output is uncomputable from a photo-inferred plan: the grid inferred from a single photograph contains NOTHING behind or beside the camera outside the frustum, so the isovist centroid is guaranteed to sit in front of the camera and 'alignment' is biased toward +1 by construction. The diagnostic case the proposal advertises (alignment near -1, main volume behind you = disorientation cue) can literally never fire. Reporting that number from Tier B would be exactly the kind of fabrication-by-artifact the panel exists to kill. With a SUPPLIED floor plan (declared extra input) drift is fully computable and defensible.
V38.fixed_computation: Two honest modes: (a) declared-plan mode — full drift vector and alignment, as sketched; (b) Tier-B photo mode — output ONLY the lateral (within-FOV, perpendicular-to-view-axis) component of drift, abstain on the longitudinal/behind-camera component and on alignment. Do not emit |d| or cos(angle) from a frustum-clipped isovist.

V39.reasons: The max-run statistic is a real, mean-proof addition and the tile computation is deterministic. All three anchors are real: Ellard's BMW Guggenheim Lab blank-facade walks (Places of the Heart, 2015), Gehl 2010's per-100m frontage grading, Gehl/Kaefer/Reigstad 2006 (Urban Design International) on walking speed past unarticulated ground floors. Two honesty conditions must be stated in the metric card: (1) EVERY anchor is about exterior street frontage experienced while walking; applying it to interior walls is a construct transfer the literature does not itself test — this must ship as proxy-grade, not as an Ellard-validated interior finding. (2) Confounds: global under-exposure or defocus makes ALL tiles low-edge-density (false full-width blank run) — needs a per-image exposure/sharpness gate before the corpus-percentile test; and without depth, run-as-fraction-of-band conflates wall length with framing distance (they already flag metric length UNKNOWN, good). Inherits V28's assumptions and dies if V28 dies. AMBER matches.
V39.fixed_computation: Add a global-image gate: abstain if whole-frame median edge density or Laplacian variance is below a floor (photo quality, not room, is then the signal). Report image-fraction and metric length as separate fields with separate abstention.

V40.reasons: Positionally distinct from existing landmark salience and the class taxonomy (LANDMARK/BLANK/OPEN) is honest about what contrast statistics can deliver. Anchors: Lynch 1960 genuinely ties path imageability to clear termini; Cullen 1961 is correctly downgraded to design-theory proxy. Conroy Dalton 2003 (Env & Behavior, least-angle route choice) is real but the gloss 'so its terminus carries orientation information' is an inference layered on her finding, not her finding — keep it hedged as stated. Main computability trap: luminance-contrast salience at the VP cannot distinguish a memorable articulated element from a bright window, specular highlight, or blown exposure — the single most common interior VP terminus IS a window, which would score maximal 'landmark' salience for reasons that are glare, not memorability. Needs the existing glare-risk bright-patch mask applied before scoring, and the aperture case routed to OPEN-CONTINUATION, which the sketch partially anticipates. VP+depth chain makes AMBER correct.
V40.fixed_computation: Intersect the terminal patch with the existing glare/bright-patch mask: if masked area exceeds a threshold, classify OPEN-CONTINUATION or ABSTAIN rather than scoring luminance contrast as landmark salience.

V41.reasons: Construct and anchors are sound: Ulrich 1983 (Altman & Wohlwill volume) does list depth/spatial definition among gross structural properties driving affective response; Kaplan & Kaplan 1989 preference work includes inferred depth/occlusion; Ko, Schiavon et al. 2020 (Building and Environment) is a real experimental window-view result on working memory and emotion — correctly cited as view STRUCTURE evidence, though note it compared view vs no-view, not layered vs unlayered view, so it supports the construct family, not this specific metric. Two computability caveats to encode: (1) monocular depth models produce smooth gradients and fail worst exactly where this metric wants to look — THROUGH glazing, where they often flatten the exterior to the glass plane or hallucinate; the 'restrict to glazing region' option is therefore the least reliable mode and should carry its own flag. (2) 'Abstain on low depth-model confidence' is currently vaporware — frozen depth models emit no calibrated confidence; it must be operationalized (e.g., left-right flip inconsistency or multi-scale disagreement) or dropped. KDE mode count can echo model artifacts, so run the one-example-before-scaling rule on a known 3-layer scene first. AMBER is right (fully rides the frozen depth model).
V41.fixed_computation: Operationalize the abstention gate as horizontal-flip depth inconsistency (or multi-crop disagreement) above a corpus-calibrated threshold; flag glazing-restricted mode as lower-evidence than whole-scene mode, not higher.

V42.reasons: Fails on both computability and citation hygiene. Computability: 'door-shaped, taller-than-wide, low-interior-luminance, floor-connected opening' is a semantic category classical CV cannot reliably deliver — dark wardrobes, full-height curtains, mirrors, and black door panels all satisfy the rule, while the most important modern egress case, a floor-to-ceiling glass door to a terrace, is BRIGHT and gets excluded by the 'exclude high windows / low luminance = navigable' logic. That is the glass-house inversion by construction: the metric would systematically score the most escapable interiors as trap-like. Distinguishing a fixed floor-connected window from an operable glass door is not recoverable from one photo by any luminance/geometry rule. Evidence: the enclosure-to-exit-decision finding is Vartanian et al. 2015, Journal of Environmental Psychology (ceiling height and perceived enclosure); Vartanian et al. 2013 PNAS is the curvilinear-vs-rectilinear CONTOUR paper — right finding, wrong paper and venue, exactly the misattribution class the panel flags. Fisher & Nasar 1992 and Appleton 1975 are real and correctly graded. The honest home for this construct is the plan's declared door/opening layer — which the proposal itself admits collapses into V19's locomotive channel, leaving no residual value for the Tier-A variant.
V42.citation_concern: Vartanian et al. 2013 (PNAS) is the contour/curvature study; the enclosure-exit-decision result is Vartanian et al. 2015, Journal of Environmental Psychology. As written the anchor misattributes finding to venue/year.
V42.fixed_computation: Only salvageable as a declared-input predicate: egress-weighted aperture salience computed from a supplied plan's door layer (openable vs fixed glazing tagged by the human), visible-from-vantage via the Tier-B sector — but that is V19's channel, so propose it there or not at all.

V43.reasons: Survives, but only at AMBER and only as a declared proxy. Computability: the three sub-measures are all genuinely deterministic, but two have named failure modes the implementation must surface. (a) The roughness proxy (mid-band RMS luminance energy) measures VISUAL texture, which confounds physical roughness with printed pattern, camera distance (spatial frequency shifts with distance — a brick wall at 6m reads smoother than fabric at 1m), and depth-of-field blur (out-of-focus regions read as smooth/glossy). (b) The gloss proxy requires specular highlights to actually be present — a glossy floor with no luminaire in the reflection geometry scores matte, and flash photography inverts this. These are photography-level confounds, not room-level facts, and should be listed as such in the attribute's uncertainty notes. What saves it: Wastiels et al. gives a real multi-predictor regression over exactly these dimensions, weight SIGNS are literature-anchored, and magnitude defaults are declared as defaults — this is the honest version of a composite (contrast V48). Riding material/plane segmentation mandates AMBER; the claimed ceiling matches. Not a duplicate: the existing warm/cool ratio is purely chromatic.

V44.reasons: Computability: sound. Per-block Gabor orientation + least-squares radial convergence fit is essentially the GIST-style expansion computation from the cited papers, is deterministic, and the cross-check against the existing VP detector is a real internal consistency test. Two failure modes must be flagged: (1) lens/framing confound — measured expansion is a function of focal length and photographer position, not just room geometry; a wide-angle shot down a corridor inflates it (same class of confound as the existing prospect metric, so acceptable if declared; normalizing by EXIF focal length when available would strengthen it); (2) radial texture patterns (sunburst flooring, radial ceiling fixtures) can produce spurious convergence fits — the VP cross-check partially guards this. Evidence: Oliva & Torralba 2001 and Ross & Oliva 2010 genuinely establish that humans' perspective/depth-gradient percepts are recoverable from these global features; the wayfinding-pull link is Gibson-theoretical and the proposal says so explicitly. That honesty is why it lives — but it also caps it: construct evidence is proxy-grade, so AMBER is the correct ceiling despite the computation being classical CV with no pretrained dependency. Dissociation from prospect (how far vs how strongly funneled) is real and well-argued.

V45.reasons: Computability: yes — SLIC + deltaE agglomerative merge + component count is deterministic and is essentially Yu, Samaras & Zelinsky's own validated pipeline, which predicted human clutter ratings better than pixel measures. Failure modes to declare: shadows and lighting gradients split single surfaces into multiple proto-objects (count inflates in raking light — a lighting confound, not an object-count fact); the count is resolution- and framing-dependent (the per-steradian normalization under declared FOV partially addresses this and must not be optional); the fixed deltaE threshold is heuristic — the promised ±20% sensitivity emission is the right honest move and should gate the confidence flag. Evidence: all three anchors are real and correctly characterized, including the honest use of Rosenholtz 2007 to justify why proto-objects rather than true objects. One mild framing stretch: Wolfe's set-size effects come from discrete search displays, and Wolfe's own scene-search work shows set size is ill-defined in real scenes — which is exactly why this is a clutter-family proxy, not a search-RT predictor; the attribute description should not promise RT prediction. Genuinely non-duplicative: it is the only discrete-chunk count in the stack, and the V7-divergence diagnostic is a nice touch. Single-lab validation + heuristic threshold = AMBER, as claimed.

V46.reasons: Computability: the pyramid mirror-NCC machinery is deterministic and implementable, and the abstention rule for oblique views is the single design decision that saves it — perspective projection destroys frontoparallel mirror symmetry, so without that rule the metric would mostly measure camera angle. Two costs to state plainly: (1) the abstention rule means this attribute will ABSTAIN on a large fraction of real interior photos (most are oblique) — acceptable but should be reported as expected coverage, not discovered later; (2) periodic patterned surfaces (wallpaper, tile) generate dense spurious symmetry peaks at fine scales that can fabricate 'nesting' — a minimum-peak-separation or periodicity suppression step is needed. Evidence: Alexander & Carey 1968, Jacobsen & Höfel 2003, and Makin/Bertamini's implicit-affect and SPN work are all real. But note the anchor's actual scope: Alexander & Carey counted subsymmetries in 1D binary strips of 7 elements; it validates 'more sub-symmetries -> better perceived goodness,' NOT the hierarchical axis-alignment structure this index specifically rewards. The nesting-specific claim rests on Alexander 2001, which is theory. The proposal flags interior extrapolation, so it survives — at AMBER, mid-rank, exactly where it put itself.
V46.citation_concern: Alexander & Carey 1968 is real but narrower than the construct: 1D binary strip patterns, subsymmetry COUNT — it does not test hierarchical axis alignment, which is anchored only by Alexander 2001 (theory). Not invented, but the index's nesting-specific component outruns its one psychophysical anchor.

V47.reasons: The cleanest of the six. Computability: PHOG histogram-intersection self-similarity is a fully specified, deterministic statistic taken directly from the source papers — no heuristic thresholds beyond bin/pyramid choices, no segmentation dependency, robust to moderate exposure changes (orientation histograms are magnitude-weighted but contrast-normalizable; specify normalization to close that hole). The dissociation argument from fractal D is correct and concrete (a striped wall and a richly self-similar scene can share box-count D while diverging sharply here). Evidence: Amirshahi et al. 2012 (PHOG self-similarity of artworks), Braun et al. 2013 (norms for architecture photographs — meaning the built-environment distribution of this statistic is actually characterized), and Redies 2007 are all real and correctly described, and the proposal correctly labels the preference link as correlational and interiors as characterized-but-not-preference-tested. That construct gap, not the computation, is what makes AMBER the right ceiling — and the proposal already says so. No duplication. Defensible to Kirsh as-is.

V48.reasons: This is the hue-affect situation wearing a factor-analytic costume, and the honest design there was abstention.
